g.data-narration: Welcome! This lecture compares Traditional (Waterfall) and Modern (Agile) project management approaches for software engineering.
Project management practices have evolved from rigid sequential methods to flexible iterative approaches, revolutionizing software development.
g.text: Traditional PM follows sequential phases; Modern PM uses iterative cycles.
Understanding both helps you choose the right approach for different projects.
This is essential for software engineering careers and exams!

g.data-narration: Traditional Project Management, also called the Waterfall model, follows a linear sequential approach with distinct phases.
g.text: • Each phase completes before next begins
• Cycles of planning, build, test, review

g.data-narration: The Waterfall model has six distinct phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, and Maintenance.
Key characteristics include extensive documentation, rigorous change control, predictive planning, milestone tracking, and a phase-gate process.
Modern project management emerged as Agile methodologies, emphasizing flexibility, collaboration, and iterative development with continuous feedback.
g.text: Agile emerged in 2001 with the Agile Manifesto, revolutionizing how software is built.
Key principle: Welcome changing requirements, even late in development.
Deliver working software frequently, from weeks to months with preference to shorter timescales.

g.data-narration: The Agile Manifesto has four core values: individuals over processes, working software over documentation, collaboration over contracts, and responding to change over plans.
Scrum is an Agile framework using time-boxed sprints (2-4 weeks) with three key roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team.
Kanban is a visual workflow method for continuous delivery without rigid time boxes, using a board with columns like To Do, In Progress, and Done.
Traditional emphasizes comprehensive documentation before development; Agile focuses on working software over extensive documentation.
Traditional has rigid change control with expensive modifications; Agile embraces change as competitive advantage with minimal overhead.
Traditional has periodic customer involvement (milestone reviews); Agile has continuous customer collaboration throughout the project lifecycle.
Use Traditional for fixed requirements, regulatory compliance, well-understood technologies, large teams, and fixed-price contracts.
Use Agile for uncertain requirements, innovation projects, startups, rapid feedback needs, and self-organizing empowered teams.
For a banking app, Traditional takes 22 months sequentially; Agile delivers working increments every 2 weeks with continuous feedback.
g.text: Customer sees product only at end

g.data-narration: Key takeaway: Traditional is predictive and plan-driven; Agile is adaptive and change-driven. Both have their place in software engineering.
Remember: Waterfall sequence, Agile four values, Scrum roles, and when to apply each. Practice scenario-based questions for best results.
